parentQuestion: A patient with diabetes has a foot wound that won't heal. Despite 6 weeks of treatment, the wound remains in a chronic inflammatory state with elevated levels of neutrophils and macrophages. Which phase of tissue repair is impaired?

scaffolds.q: Tissue repair has three phases: inflammation, proliferation, remodeling.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Each phase must complete before the next can proceed.

scaffolds.q: Inflammation is the FIRST phase - cleanup before rebuilding.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Immune cells (neutrophils, macrophages) clear debris and fight infection.

scaffolds.q: Proliferation is the SECOND phase - building new tissue scaffold.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Granulation tissue forms only AFTER inflammation resolves.

scaffolds.q: Inflammation must RESOLVE before proliferation can begin.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! You can't build while you're still in cleanup mode. Sequential phases.

scaffolds.q: Chronic inflammation means the wound is STUCK in the inflammatory phase.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! 6 weeks of persistent neutrophils and macrophages = inflammation that won't resolve.

scaffolds.q: Diabetes impairs wound healing through poor circulation and immune dysfunction.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! High glucose damages blood vessels and impairs immune cell function.

scaffolds.q: If inflammation can't resolve, the wound can't transition to proliferation.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! The wound stays trapped in phase 1. No granulation tissue, no healing.

scaffolds.q: This diabetic wound is stuck in INFLAMMATION - it can't transition to proliferation.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Persistent neutrophils and macrophages show ongoing inflammation. The phase 1→2 transition is broken.

scaffolds.q: The answer is: transition from INFLAMMATORY to PROLIFERATIVE phase is impaired.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Chronic inflammation = stuck in phase 1. The wound can't move forward to build new tissue.
